scope: The most common derivations: combining existing features through simple arithmetic (ratios, differences, products) to make relationships explicit for the model.
key_concepts: Derived features provide the model with explicit signals that it might otherwise struggle to learn or would require a more complex model architecture to approximate.
Differences (subtraction) measure the gap or change between two related quantities, often representing growth, deficit, or elapsed time.
Ratios (division) capture relative proportions, normalizing absolute values to create comparable metrics across different scales.
Interaction features (multiplication) represent the combined effect of two or more features, particularly when their joint impact is not merely additive.

definitions.definition: A new feature created by multiplying two or more existing features to capture their combined effect.
A feature constructed by applying mathematical or logical operations to one or more raw features.

examples.description: Difference: Subtracting "expenses" from "income" to create a "net profit" feature.
Ratio: Dividing "total debt" by "total assets" to calculate a "debt-to-asset ratio", which is much more informative for credit risk than the absolute numbers.
Interaction: Multiplying "house age" by "requires renovation flag" to create a feature that captures the compounded negative effect on property price.

relations.builds_toward: Polynomial features in ch06_s02, which is a generalization of interaction features.
relations.prerequisites: Basic understanding of numeric representations from Chapter 3.

exclusions: Mathematical proofs of why linear models fail at XOR problems (keep it intuitive).
